title: Te Whakatau Tātai o Te Awa: The Mathematics of River Restoration
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will investigate the mathematical principles behind restoring Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the local river that holds deep significance for Ngāti Kahungunu. The river's name references the native shrub (mangakōtukutuku) that once flourished along its banks, and its restoration represents both environmental and cultural revitalization. Students will connect mathematical concepts to the traditional knowledge of river management and the current community-led restoration project.
learningObjectives: Calculate and compare flow rates and water quality measurements using ratios and percentages to assess the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Apply geometric principles to design and scale riparian planting zones that maximize biodiversity and erosion control
Analyze historical and current environmental data using statistical measures to evaluate the effectiveness of restoration efforts
Create mathematical models that integrate mātauranga Māori perspectives with scientific data to propose sustainable river management solutions

activities.title: Te Awa Tātai: River Mathematics Investigation
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to collect water flow measurements, calculate cross-sectional areas, and determine flow rates using mathematical formulas. They compare current measurements with historical data provided by local kaumātua.

activities.title: Whakairo Whenua: Geometric Planting Design
activities.description: Using geometric principles, students design scaled planting plans for the riverbanks. They calculate areas, perimeters, and optimal planting patterns that reflect traditional Māori land management practices while maximizing biodiversity.

activities.title: Tātai Tatauranga: Statistical Analysis of Restoration
activities.description: Students analyze water quality data collected over the past decade, calculating mean, median, mode, and range to identify trends and correlations between restoration efforts and environmental improvements.

activities.title: Hōtaka Whakaora: Mathematical Modeling Challenge
activities.description: Working in groups, students create mathematical models that predict the river's recovery timeline based on different restoration scenarios, incorporating both scientific data and cultural priorities from community consultations.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and environmental experts for cultural guidance
Hawke's Bay Regional Council river monitoring data and GIS mapping tools
Traditional Māori measurement tools (kumi, maro) and modern equivalents
Local restoration project plans and community consultation reports

tasks: Complete a detailed mathematical report analyzing the current state of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Design and present a scaled restoration plan using geometric principles
Create and justify a mathematical model predicting restoration outcomes
Reflect on how mathematical understanding supports cultural preservation

nzcAlignment: Mathematics and Statistics: Number and Algebra - Patterns and relationships
Mathematics and Statistics: Geometry and Measurement - Position and orientation
Mathematics and Statistics: Statistics - Statistical investigation
Tikanga-ā-Iwi: Continuity and Change - Understanding how cultural practices evolve
